enrichment.short_description: Open-source, self-hosted federated personal profile that lets you publish content and connect with the fediverse via ActivityPub and Diaspora.

long_form_blocks.heading: Overview
paragraphs: Socialhome is an open-source, self-hosted platform that combines a personal profile page with social networking features. Content you post — text, images, and rich Markdown or HTML/CSS/JS for trusted users — is pinned to your own profile and federates out to other users and platforms.

Rather than building a closed network, Socialhome speaks the ActivityPub and Diaspora federation protocols, so posts, replies, and shares can reach and be received from other fediverse software (such as Mastodon, Friendica, and Diaspora) as well as other Socialhome instances.
The project is maintained on Codeberg under the AGPLv3 license, with a Python/Django backend and a Vue.js frontend, and can be deployed via Docker.

long_form_blocks.heading: Who It's For
paragraphs: Socialhome suits individuals and communities who want a personal, content-first home base on the fediverse rather than a chronological feed-only timeline, and who are comfortable running or joining a self-hosted instance.

features.title: Federated profile
features.description: Your profile is the home for your content, federating to contacts and other fediverse servers over ActivityPub and Diaspora.

features.title: Rich content authoring
features.description: Posts support Markdown, and trusted users can embed HTML, JS, and CSS for custom layouts.

features.title: Content visibility controls
features.description: Choose the visibility of individual posts, from public to limited audiences.

features.title: Streams and tags
features.description: Follow tags and streams to organize incoming federated content by topic.

features.title: Self-hosted deployment
features.description: Runs as a self-hosted Django application, with Docker images provided for installation.

pros: Fully open source (AGPLv3) with no vendor lock-in
Broad interoperability with the wider fediverse via ActivityPub and Diaspora
Lets users own and control their own content on their own server
Actively maintained codebase on Codeberg
cons: Requires self-hosting knowledge (server, Docker, updates)
Smaller community than mainstream fediverse platforms like Mastodon
No official hosted/managed offering from the project itself

faqs.question: Is Socialhome free to use?
faqs.answer: Yes. Socialhome is free, open-source software licensed under AGPLv3. You run your own instance, so the only cost is your own server hosting.

faqs.question: Can Socialhome talk to Mastodon or Diaspora?
faqs.answer: Yes. Socialhome federates using both the ActivityPub protocol (used by Mastodon and many other fediverse apps) and the Diaspora protocol.

faqs.question: Do I need to run my own server?
faqs.answer: You can either self-host an instance yourself using the provided Docker images, or join an existing open-registration Socialhome instance run by someone else.

use_cases.title: Personal fediverse home page
use_cases.description: Use Socialhome as a public, content-rich personal profile that federates posts across the fediverse.
use_cases.audience: Individuals who want a self-owned social presence

use_cases.title: Community instance
use_cases.description: Run a shared Socialhome instance for a group or community that wants to interact with the wider fediverse.

enrichment.seo_meta_description: Socialhome is an open-source, self-hosted federated profile platform that connects to the fediverse via ActivityPub and Diaspora.

enrichment.short_description: Open-source community platform blending forum Q&A, blog comments, and chat, available self-hosted or as hosted plans.

long_form_blocks.heading: Overview
paragraphs: Talkyard is an open-source community discussion platform created by Magnus Lindberg that brings together features found across StackOverflow-style Q&A, Slack-like chat, Reddit-style voting, Discourse-style forums, and Disqus-style blog comments in one system.

It can be self-hosted on your own servers with automatic updates, Single Sign-On (including OpenID Connect), and API access, or used via Talkyard's hosted plans. A single installation can support multiple communities.
Common uses include internal coworker forums and knowledge bases, idea/feature voting boards, and embeddable blog comment widgets, all built around threaded discussions with upvoting.

long_form_blocks.heading: Who It's For
paragraphs: Talkyard suits teams and companies that want an internal knowledge base or Q&A forum, website owners who want an open-source Disqus alternative for blog comments, and organizations that want ideation/voting boards without relying on closed SaaS platforms.

features.title: Forum and Q&A
features.description: Threaded discussions with instant answers and upvoting, similar to StackOverflow-style knowledge bases.

features.title: Blog comments
features.description: Embeddable comment widget for blogs with built-in user accounts.

features.title: Ideation and voting
features.description: Vote-based idea and feature-request boards designed to surface good ideas and reduce groupthink.

features.title: Single Sign-On and API
features.description: OpenID Connect login, Single Sign-On, and API access included across plans.

features.title: Self-hosting
features.description: Deploy Talkyard on your own infrastructure with automatic updates and daily off-site backups.

pros: Open source (AGPLv3) and self-hostable at no license cost
Combines forum, Q&A, chat, and comments in one platform
Built-in SSO/OIDC and API access on all plans
One installation can host multiple separate communities
cons: Hosted plans have per-plan storage and post-volume caps
Built-in chat is more basic than dedicated chat tools like Slack
Self-hosting requires managing your own server and upgrades

faqs.question: Is Talkyard free?
faqs.answer: Talkyard is open source and free to self-host. Hosted plans start at a low monthly price for small communities, with paid tiers for higher admin counts, storage, and volume.

faqs.question: Can Talkyard replace Disqus for blog comments?
faqs.answer: Yes, Talkyard offers an embeddable, open-source blog comments widget as an alternative to Disqus.

faqs.question: Does Talkyard support Single Sign-On?
faqs.answer: Yes, all plans include OpenID Connect login and Single Sign-On support.

use_cases.title: Internal knowledge base
use_cases.description: Run a Q&A style forum where coworkers ask questions and upvote the best answers.

use_cases.title: Blog comments
use_cases.description: Embed Talkyard's comment widget on a blog as an open-source Disqus alternative.

use_cases.title: Feature idea board
use_cases.description: Collect and vote on product ideas from customers or team members.

features: Up to 1,500 new replies and 20 new pages per month
Up to 1,500 new replies and pages per month

enrichment.seo_meta_description: Talkyard is an open-source community platform combining forum Q&A, blog comments, and chat — self-hosted for free or via hosted plans from €1.90/month.

enrichment.short_description: Decentralized, self-hosted microblogging ecosystem built on the plain-text twtxt protocol, with no central server or company.

long_form_blocks.heading: Overview
paragraphs: Yarn.social is a decentralized ecosystem for social micro-blogging built around the twtxt specification — a minimal plain-text format where each post is a single line containing a timestamp and a message, stored in a `twtxt.txt` file hosted wherever the author chooses.

There is no central Yarn.social server to sign up for. Instead, the project provides open-source software — including twtd (a single-user feed server) and yarnd (a multi-user 'pod' server) — that anyone can self-host to publish and follow feeds.

The ecosystem also bridges to other formats, including ActivityPub-based fediverse platforms and RSS/Atom feeds, and includes a search/crawler tool for discovering feeds across the network. It is created and maintained by James Mills (prologic), operator of the twtxt.net pod.

long_form_blocks.heading: Who It's For
paragraphs: Yarn.social suits people who want to own their social posts as plain text on infrastructure they control, and developers or communities interested in a lightweight, decentralized alternative to mainstream microblogging platforms.

features.title: Plain-text feeds
features.description: Posts live in a simple twtxt.txt file that can be hosted on any web server you control.

features.title: yarnd pod server
features.description: Self-hosted, Docker-deployable server for running a multi-user Yarn.social community pod.

features.title: twtd single-user server
features.description: Lightweight server for publishing a single personal twtxt feed.

features.title: Federation bridges
features.description: Interoperates with ActivityPub-based fediverse apps and RSS/Atom feeds.

features.title: Feed discovery
features.description: Search and crawler tooling for finding and following feeds across independently run pods.

pros: No central company or server — content lives in plain text you control
Fully open source under AGPLv3
Lightweight protocol that's easy to inspect and self-host
Bridges to the wider fediverse and RSS/Atom
cons: Requires self-hosting to participate fully
Much smaller community than mainstream social networks
Plain-text protocol has fewer built-in features than full social platforms

faqs.question: Do I need to sign up for a Yarn.social account?
faqs.answer: No. There is no central Yarn.social service to sign up for — you either self-host your own feed/pod or join an independently run pod such as twtxt.net.

faqs.question: What is twtxt?
faqs.answer: twtxt is the minimal, plain-text protocol behind Yarn.social: each post is one line in a text file containing a timestamp and a message.

faqs.question: Is Yarn.social free?
faqs.answer: Yes, the software is free and open source (AGPLv3); the project accepts optional donations via OpenCollective.

use_cases.title: Self-hosted microblog
use_cases.description: Publish short posts from a personal twtxt feed hosted on your own web server.
use_cases.audience: Individuals who want to own their posts

use_cases.title: Community pod
use_cases.description: Run a yarnd server as a shared community pod for multiple users.

enrichment.seo_meta_description: yarn.social is a decentralized, self-hosted microblogging ecosystem built on the plain-text twtxt protocol, with no central server or company.

enrichment.short_description: Open-source, self-hosted WebRTC gateway server from Meetecho for building custom real-time audio, video, and data applications.

long_form_blocks.heading: Overview
paragraphs: Janus (Janus Gateway) is a general-purpose, open-source WebRTC server developed by Meetecho, written in C for a lightweight footprint. Rather than shipping as a finished conferencing app, it acts as middleware that relays RTP/RTCP media and JSON signaling between browsers and server-side logic.

Its architecture is plugin-based: Janus itself is protocol-agnostic, and specific capabilities — such as echo tests, conference bridges (videoroom), media recording/streaming, and SIP gateways — are provided by loadable server-side plugins, so deployments only include the functionality they need.

Because it is self-hosted and highly modular, Janus is commonly used as a building block for custom video conferencing, streaming, and real-time communication products rather than as an out-of-the-box end-user application.

long_form_blocks.heading: Who It's For
paragraphs: Janus is aimed at developers and organizations that need to build custom WebRTC-based communication features — video conferencing, streaming, or SIP integration — on infrastructure they control, rather than end users looking for a ready-made meeting app.

features.title: Plugin architecture
features.description: Core gateway stays lightweight; functionality such as video rooms, recording, or SIP is added via server-side plugins.

features.title: VideoRoom plugin
features.description: Multi-party audio/video conference bridge plugin, one of the most widely used Janus plugins.

features.title: SIP gateway plugin
features.description: Bridges WebRTC clients to SIP-based telephony infrastructure.

features.title: Recording and streaming
features.description: Plugins for recording sessions and streaming pre-recorded or live media.

features.title: Lightweight C core
features.description: Designed to run efficiently from cloud servers down to small embedded devices.

pros: Free, open-source, and self-hosted with no per-user licensing cost
Highly modular plugin system keeps deployments lean
Scales from small embedded setups to large cloud deployments
Actively maintained by Meetecho, its original developers
cons: Not a turnkey end-user product — requires development work to build an application on top
Operating and scaling a production WebRTC server requires networking/ops expertise
Documentation assumes familiarity with WebRTC concepts

faqs.question: Is Janus a video conferencing app I can use directly?
faqs.answer: Not by itself. Janus is a WebRTC server/toolkit — developers use its plugins (such as VideoRoom) as building blocks for their own conferencing or streaming applications.

faqs.question: Is Janus free?
faqs.answer: Yes, Janus is free, open-source software that you self-host.

faqs.question: Who maintains Janus?
faqs.answer: Janus is developed and maintained by Meetecho, the organization that originally created it.

use_cases.title: Custom video conferencing backend
use_cases.description: Use the VideoRoom plugin as the media server for a custom-built conferencing product.

use_cases.title: WebRTC-to-SIP bridging
use_cases.description: Connect browser-based WebRTC clients to existing SIP telephony systems.

enrichment.seo_meta_description: Janus is a free, open-source, self-hosted WebRTC gateway from Meetecho used to build custom video, audio, and SIP communication applications.

enrichment.short_description: Open-source, self-hosted, end-to-end encrypted peer-to-peer video calling app for 1-to-1 'cam-to-cam' calls, embeddable via iframe.

long_form_blocks.heading: Overview
paragraphs: MiroTalk C2C ('cam-to-cam') is a self-hosted, open-source WebRTC video calling app built by Miroslav Pejic for one-to-one real-time communication. Unlike the broader MiroTalk conferencing products, each C2C room is limited to exactly two participants, which is used as a security and simplicity measure.

Calls are peer-to-peer and end-to-end encrypted, with screen sharing supported alongside camera video. The app requires only a room name to join and can be embedded into any website with a simple iframe.

It is part of the wider MiroTalk suite (which also includes P2P, SFU, and other products) and is released under the AGPLv3 license, with a commercial license also available for closed-source use via CodeCanyon.

long_form_blocks.heading: Who It's For
paragraphs: MiroTalk C2C fits use cases that need a simple, private, two-person video call embedded into a site or app — such as 1:1 support calls, interviews, or telehealth-style consultations — run on self-hosted infrastructure.

features.title: Two-participant rooms
features.description: Each room is strictly limited to two people, designed for focused 1-to-1 calls.

features.title: End-to-end encryption
features.description: Peer-to-peer WebRTC calls with end-to-end encryption between the two participants.

features.title: Screen sharing
features.description: Share your screen alongside camera video during a call.

features.title: Iframe embedding
features.description: Drop the calling widget into any website via a simple iframe.

features.title: Self-hosted deployment
features.description: Deployable via Node.js or Docker/docker-compose on your own server.

pros: Free and open source (AGPLv3), self-hostable
No account or download required for participants
Simple, focused use case (1-to-1 calls) with minimal setup
Easy to embed into an existing website
cons: Hard two-participant limit, unlike other MiroTalk products
Peer-to-peer architecture depends on both users' network conditions
Requires self-hosting and server maintenance

faqs.question: How many people can join a MiroTalk C2C call?
faqs.answer: MiroTalk C2C rooms are limited to exactly two participants; it is designed specifically for 1-to-1 calls.

faqs.question: Is MiroTalk C2C free?
faqs.answer: Yes, it is free and open source under AGPLv3. A commercial license is also available for closed-source/commercial use via CodeCanyon.

faqs.question: Can I embed MiroTalk C2C on my website?
faqs.answer: Yes, it can be embedded into any website using a simple iframe.

use_cases.title: 1:1 support or consultation calls
use_cases.description: Embed a private, two-person video call directly into a support or booking page.

use_cases.title: Private personal calls
use_cases.description: Self-host a simple, encrypted video call room for two people without third-party services.

enrichment.seo_meta_description: MiroTalk C2C is a free, open-source, self-hosted peer-to-peer video calling app for encrypted 1-to-1 cam-to-cam calls, embeddable via iframe.

enrichment.short_description: Free, open-source, self-hosted peer-to-peer video conferencing app with screen sharing, chat, whiteboard, and recording.

long_form_blocks.heading: Overview
paragraphs: MiroTalk P2P is a free, open-source, browser-based video conferencing app built on peer-to-peer WebRTC architecture, requiring no download, plugin, or login to join a call.

Because media flows directly between participants rather than through a media server, MiroTalk P2P is aimed at smaller calls where it can offer low latency and high video quality (up to 8K/60fps) with end-to-end encryption. It includes public and private chat, an interactive whiteboard, meeting recording, and file sharing over DTLS-encrypted transfer.
It is released under the AGPLv3 license and is self-hosted via Node.js or Docker; a commercial license is also available through CodeCanyon for closed-source use.

long_form_blocks.heading: Who It's For
paragraphs: MiroTalk P2P suits small teams or individuals who want a free, self-hosted, privacy-focused video calling tool for small-group calls without relying on a third-party SaaS provider.

features.title: Peer-to-peer calling
features.description: Media streams flow directly between participants for low latency and no central media server.

features.title: Screen and webcam sharing
features.description: Share your screen or webcam at up to 8K resolution and 60fps.

features.title: Interactive whiteboard
features.description: Built-in collaborative whiteboard for drawing and explaining ideas during a call.

features.title: Meeting recording
features.description: Record screen, webcam, and audio directly from the browser.

features.title: REST API
features.description: API access for integrating room creation and management into other applications.

features.title: AI chat assistant
features.description: Built-in ChatGPT-based assistant available during calls.

pros: Free and open source (AGPLv3), fully self-hostable
No login or download required to join a call
Low-latency peer-to-peer architecture for small groups
Feature-rich for a free tool: whiteboard, recording, chat, file sharing
cons: Peer-to-peer architecture scales poorly for larger group calls (unlike SFU-based tools)
Call quality depends on each participant's own network and device
Requires self-hosting and server maintenance

faqs.question: Is MiroTalk P2P free?
faqs.answer: Yes, it is free and open source under AGPLv3, with an optional commercial license available via CodeCanyon for closed-source use.

faqs.question: How is MiroTalk P2P different from MiroTalk SFU?
faqs.answer: MiroTalk P2P connects participants directly to each other (peer-to-peer), which suits smaller calls, while MiroTalk SFU routes media through a Selective Forwarding Unit server, which scales better to larger meetings.

faqs.question: Do participants need to create an account?
faqs.answer: No, MiroTalk P2P can be joined directly in a browser without a login or download.

use_cases.title: Small-team video calls
use_cases.description: Host quick, private calls for small groups without a third-party conferencing service.

use_cases.title: Embedded calling widget
use_cases.description: Use the REST API to integrate MiroTalk P2P calling into another application or website.

enrichment.seo_meta_description: MiroTalk P2P is a free, open-source, self-hosted peer-to-peer video conferencing app with screen sharing, whiteboard, recording, and a REST API.

enrichment.short_description: Free, open-source, self-hosted video conferencing platform built on a Mediasoup SFU for scalable group calls with recording and streaming.

long_form_blocks.heading: Overview
paragraphs: MiroTalk SFU is a free, open-source, browser-based video conferencing platform that uses Mediasoup as its Selective Forwarding Unit (SFU), allowing it to scale to larger group meetings than the peer-to-peer MiroTalk product.

It supports up to 8K/60fps video, screen sharing, echo-cancelled audio, chat, meeting recording, a collaborative whiteboard, file sharing, RTMP streaming, and virtual backgrounds, and offers optional ChatGPT integration and AI avatars. Media streams are encrypted with SRTP.
Like the rest of the MiroTalk suite, it is released under AGPLv3 for self-hosting, with a commercial license available via CodeCanyon, and exposes a REST API for building integrations.

long_form_blocks.heading: Who It's For
paragraphs: MiroTalk SFU suits organizations that need self-hosted group video conferencing at larger scale than a pure peer-to-peer tool can support, including teams that want streaming or recording built in.

features.title: Mediasoup SFU architecture
features.description: Routes media through a Selective Forwarding Unit server, allowing larger group calls than peer-to-peer connections.

features.title: RTMP streaming
features.description: Stream meetings out to RTMP-compatible platforms.

features.title: Virtual backgrounds and AI avatars
features.description: Apply virtual backgrounds or AI-generated avatars during calls.

features.title: Recording and whiteboard
features.description: Record meetings and collaborate on a built-in shared whiteboard.

features.title: REST API
features.description: Programmatic access for creating and managing rooms from other applications.

pros: Free and open source (AGPLv3), fully self-hostable
SFU architecture scales to larger group calls than peer-to-peer tools
Unlimited rooms/users and no call-time limits when self-hosted
Rich feature set including streaming, recording, and whiteboard
cons: Requires more server resources than the peer-to-peer MiroTalk product
Self-hosting and scaling the SFU requires ops expertise
AI features depend on external services (e.g. ChatGPT) being configured

faqs.question: Is MiroTalk SFU free?
faqs.answer: Yes, it is free and open source under AGPLv3, with unlimited rooms and users when self-hosted; a commercial license is also available via CodeCanyon.

faqs.question: How is MiroTalk SFU different from MiroTalk P2P?
faqs.answer: MiroTalk SFU routes media through a Mediasoup Selective Forwarding Unit server, which scales to larger group calls, whereas MiroTalk P2P connects participants directly to each other and suits smaller calls.

faqs.question: Does MiroTalk SFU support streaming?
faqs.answer: Yes, it supports RTMP streaming of meetings to external platforms.

use_cases.title: Self-hosted group video conferencing
use_cases.description: Replace a commercial conferencing SaaS with a self-hosted SFU-based alternative for team meetings.

use_cases.title: Live streamed events
use_cases.description: Stream a meeting or webinar out via RTMP to other platforms.

enrichment.seo_meta_description: MiroTalk SFU is a free, open-source, self-hosted video conferencing platform built on Mediasoup for scalable group calls with recording and streaming.

enrichment.short_description: Open-source, self-hosted WebRTC conferencing platform built on LiveKit and NATS, with recording, streaming, and AI meeting tools.

long_form_blocks.heading: Overview
paragraphs: plugNmeet is an open-source, self-hosted WebRTC conferencing platform maintained by Mynaparrot, built to be embedded into websites and applications rather than used only as a standalone app. Its backend is written in Go and runs on LiveKit and NATS, deploying as a single binary designed for horizontal scaling.

It offers HD recording, RTMP/WHIP streaming, real-time collaboration tools such as whiteboards and shared notepads, end-to-end encryption, and adaptive quality for varying network conditions. A newer 'AI Meeting Agent' feature adds live translation, transcription, and meeting summaries.
Because it is self-hosted and on-premise, plugNmeet is positioned as an alternative to third-party conferencing SaaS for organizations that want to keep meeting data under their own control.

long_form_blocks.heading: Who It's For
paragraphs: plugNmeet suits businesses and platforms that want to embed customizable, self-hosted video conferencing directly into their own product, and organizations that need on-premise control over meeting data.

features.title: LiveKit/NATS backend
features.description: Built in Go on LiveKit and NATS, deployed as a single binary designed for horizontal scaling.

features.title: HD recording and streaming
features.description: Record meetings in HD and stream out via RTMP or WHIP.

features.title: Collaboration tools
features.description: Built-in whiteboard and shared notepad for real-time collaboration during meetings.

features.title: AI Meeting Agent
features.description: Optional AI features for live translation, transcription, and meeting summaries.

features.title: End-to-end encryption
features.description: Encrypts meeting media for privacy.

pros: Free, open source, and self-hostable on-premise
Designed to be embedded/customized into other products
Built for horizontal scaling as a single Go binary
Includes AI transcription/translation and summary tools
cons: Requires self-hosting and infrastructure management (Go, LiveKit, NATS)
AI meeting features likely require additional configuration/external services
Smaller community than mainstream conferencing platforms

faqs.question: Is plugNmeet free?
faqs.answer: Yes, plugNmeet is open-source software that you self-host on your own infrastructure.

faqs.question: What is plugNmeet built on?
faqs.answer: Its backend is written in Go and uses LiveKit and NATS, deploying as a single binary designed for horizontal scaling.

faqs.question: Can plugNmeet transcribe or summarize meetings?
faqs.answer: Yes, its AI Meeting Agent feature can provide live translation, transcription, and meeting summaries.

use_cases.title: Embedded conferencing
use_cases.description: Integrate self-hosted video conferencing directly into an existing website or application.

use_cases.title: On-premise meetings
use_cases.description: Run video meetings entirely on infrastructure the organization controls.

enrichment.seo_meta_description: plugNmeet is an open-source, self-hosted WebRTC conferencing platform built on LiveKit and NATS with recording, streaming, and AI meeting tools.

enrichment.short_description: Open-source, self-hosted XMPP server, MQTT broker, and SIP service from ProcessOne, built for large-scale real-time messaging.

long_form_blocks.heading: Overview
paragraphs: ejabberd is an open-source messaging platform built in Erlang by ProcessOne, functioning as an XMPP server, MQTT broker, and SIP/WebRTC service for real-time communication. It has been developed by ProcessOne since 2002.

It supports over 400 XMPP extensions (including MUC group chat, MAM message archiving, PubSub, message carbons, push notifications, and OMEMO encryption), along with MQTT for IoT use cases, SIP/WebRTC for voice and video, and Matrix federation. Built-in clustering, REST APIs, web administration, and command-line tools are included.

The Community Server is free and open source under GPLv2. ProcessOne also offers a paid Business Edition with LTS support and enterprise modules, and Fluux, a managed SaaS hosting option. ejabberd is used at large scale by organizations including WhatsApp (in its early infrastructure) and others.

long_form_blocks.heading: Who It's For
paragraphs: ejabberd suits organizations that need a robust, self-hosted messaging backend — for chat apps, IoT device messaging, or SIP/WebRTC voice and video — at scales ranging from small deployments to millions of concurrent connections.

features.title: XMPP server
features.description: Full-featured XMPP server supporting over 400 protocol extensions including group chat, message archiving, and push notifications.

features.title: MQTT broker
features.description: Built-in MQTT support for IoT device messaging.

features.title: SIP/WebRTC service
features.description: Voice and video signaling support alongside messaging.

features.title: Matrix federation
features.description: Federates with Matrix-based messaging networks.

features.title: Clustering and scalability
features.description: Supports clustering for fault tolerance and massive concurrent connection counts.

features.title: REST API and admin tools
features.description: REST APIs, a web admin interface, and command-line tools for management.

pros: Free, open-source Community Server (GPLv2) with no vendor lock-in
Extremely mature project with over two decades of development
Proven at very large scale in production
Broad protocol support: XMPP, MQTT, SIP/WebRTC, Matrix
cons: Business Edition and Fluux hosted plans are priced for enterprise budgets
Erlang-based stack has a smaller talent pool than more common languages
Advanced clustering/scaling setups require Erlang/OTP operational knowledge

faqs.question: Is ejabberd free?
faqs.answer: The ejabberd Community Server is free and open source under GPLv2. ProcessOne also offers a paid Business Edition and a Fluux managed hosting option.

faqs.question: What protocols does ejabberd support?
faqs.answer: ejabberd supports XMPP (with over 400 extensions), MQTT, SIP/WebRTC, and Matrix federation.

faqs.question: How well does ejabberd scale?
faqs.answer: ProcessOne states ejabberd has handled over 2 million concurrent sessions on a single node in production deployments, with clustering for further scale.

faqs.question: Who makes ejabberd?
faqs.answer: ejabberd is developed by ProcessOne, a Paris-based messaging infrastructure company that has maintained it since 2002.

use_cases.title: Chat application backend
use_cases.description: Power the messaging backend for a chat app using XMPP with self-hosted infrastructure.

use_cases.title: IoT device messaging
use_cases.description: Use the built-in MQTT broker to handle messaging between IoT devices.

use_cases.title: Enterprise messaging at scale
use_cases.description: Run large-scale, clustered real-time messaging infrastructure with LTS support via the Business Edition.

enrichment.seo_meta_description: ejabberd is an open-source, self-hosted XMPP server, MQTT broker, and SIP service from ProcessOne, free as Community Server or paid as Business Edition.

enrichment.short_description: Open-source, self-hosted, Erlang-based XMPP messaging server from Erlang Solutions built for scalable instant messaging.

long_form_blocks.heading: Overview
paragraphs: MongooseIM is a free, open-source instant messaging server built on Erlang by Erlang Solutions, a London-headquartered company founded in 1999 that also offers Erlang/OTP consulting and support services.

It functions primarily as an XMPP server, additionally exposing REST, MQTT, and AMQP interfaces, with a GraphQL admin API and TOML-based configuration. Features include end-to-end encryption support, group chat, delivery confirmations, push notifications via the companion MongoosePush service, and video/voice signaling coordination.

MongooseIM is designed for horizontal and vertical scaling across distributed data centers, and Erlang Solutions markets it toward organizations needing to support large numbers of concurrent connections, including in healthcare and fintech settings.

long_form_blocks.heading: Who It's For
paragraphs: MongooseIM suits engineering teams that need a self-hosted, horizontally scalable XMPP-based messaging backend, particularly in regulated industries like healthcare and fintech where data control and GDPR compliance matter.

features.title: XMPP server core
features.description: Full XMPP protocol support as the primary messaging backbone.

features.title: Multi-protocol interfaces
features.description: Additional REST, MQTT, and AMQP interfaces alongside XMPP.

features.title: GraphQL admin API
features.description: Administer and manage the server via a GraphQL API, configured through TOML files.

features.title: Push notifications
features.description: Mobile push notification support via the companion MongoosePush service.

features.title: Horizontal and vertical scaling
features.description: Designed to scale across distributed data centers to support millions of concurrent users.

pros: Free and open source, self-hostable
Built by Erlang Solutions, an established Erlang/OTP specialist company
Supports multiple protocols (XMPP, REST, MQTT, AMQP) beyond raw XMPP
Designed for large-scale, distributed deployments
cons: Erlang-based stack has a smaller developer talent pool than more mainstream languages
No published self-serve pricing for professional support/consulting
Requires self-hosting and operational expertise to run at scale

faqs.question: Is MongooseIM free?
faqs.answer: Yes, MongooseIM is a free and open-source product that you self-host on your own infrastructure.

faqs.question: Who develops MongooseIM?
faqs.answer: MongooseIM is developed and maintained by Erlang Solutions, a London-based Erlang/OTP consultancy founded in 1999.

faqs.question: What protocols does MongooseIM support?
faqs.answer: MongooseIM is primarily an XMPP server, and also exposes REST, MQTT, and AMQP interfaces, plus a GraphQL admin API.

use_cases.title: Large-scale messaging backend
use_cases.description: Run a horizontally scaled XMPP messaging backend for a chat product with many concurrent users.

use_cases.title: Regulated-industry messaging
use_cases.description: Self-host messaging infrastructure to meet data-control and GDPR requirements in healthcare or fintech.

enrichment.seo_meta_description: MongooseIM is a free, open-source, self-hosted XMPP messaging server from Erlang Solutions, built for scalable, multi-protocol instant messaging.
